@Ethics And Aesthetics Time/Money a the solution to a bug may take so long that it would cause delays in other areas of the game pushing the whole project behind schedule. Also dedicating employee time to bug fixes instead of game development can be costly especially if the solution is still unkown
Finding bugs is rarely the problem in game development. Is having the resources to fix them.
@guilhermesfk  and then the bug might become a hydra that becomes 100 other bugs once fixed

@guilhermesfk  resources are not a problem when you have a multi million pound budget, it is the unwillingness to prioritise them that is the issue. (old) Blizzard used to relase things 'when ready' and spend a lot of time putting 'polish' on. In normal terms that means fixing bugs and testing the gameplay :)
I'm kind of surprised they never did that.

@MrDj232  Neural networks don't actually know things, for the most part they test a general way to do a specific type of task. It's like a 2 year old fitting shapes into a hole. Give that 2 year old some time and he'll get there. In a sense, neural networks are usually much stupider than even a toddler as they cannot predict an outcome, only act in a way they learned when the situation is X, and often they stop optimizing further when the outcome is sufficient, when they could be faster. They can however memorize a staggering amount of data.
